Let's start by returning to our description of the validate process from the original TDB Fall 2020 Enrollment Email series. During the Discover phase, we defined a minimum viable audience, MVA, that we seek to serve, and identified one to three significant problems, or meaningful desires, we can charge money to help that audience solve, or fulfill. When we listen closely to the subtext, the nuances, the between the lines of what our MVA are saying about the problems they care about solving, ideas emerge, often hidden within the noise. It's our job to find these insights, then highlight and express them in our offers. Validation begins when we present our ideas for solutions directly to our minimum viable audience to assess their reactions and gauge their level of interest. Often, this is where our initial attempt at explaining an offer happens. An offer is the marketing expression of a product or service at a specific point in time. It is not the product or service itself. When we share a simple expression of an offer with an MVA, the goal is to gather data quickly that confirms we're on the right path, or that we've missed the mark and need to brainstorm new ideas. We want to know, in advance, that our ideas for solutions are likely to produce positive results before we invest time and energy to build the supporting infrastructure. The last thing we want to do is create ads, websites, landing pages, emails, and sales infrastructure for offers no one wants or cares about. Validate, PARTA will focus on this part. Validation begins when we present our ideas for solutions directly to our minimum viable audience to assess their reactions and gauge their level of interest. To do that, we need to have some ideas about who is included in our MVA, broad strokes, and we need to have a reasonably clear understanding of the value we can offer that MVA as it relates to the jobs they want to accomplish, pains they want to avoid, and gains they seek to acquire. Opportunity VIN This is a perfect opportunity to mention that the line between discover and validate is fuzzy. In fact, all of the elements of the TDB flywheel are more of a spectrum than a series of clearly delineated paint by number steps. Let's consider an example from our experience with Project X, our example business for TDB. Initially, we researched a very broad audience of people who expressed interest and shared their opinions about creativity. We know that's not our MVA, but we think our MVA is within that larger audience. As we began to compile themes, we started to notice overlap between what we had found researching creativity in general and another area of significant interest for both of us, personal knowledge management, PKM. Creativity VIN Many of the jobs, pains, and gains we had uncovered in our initial creativity research applied perfectly to the jobs, pains, and gains that PKM addresses, including producing and generating ideas, solving problems, making better decisions, capturing and organizing research, being more productive, generating aha moments on demand. It seems reasonable to believe that people who are interested in PKM also are interested in creativity. However, the reverse isn't necessarily true, i.e., people interested in creativity are also interested in PKM. Knowing that, we can be confident that we have found a smaller audience to begin to focus our attention on. Another factor that influenced our thinking is that we know the PKM landscape reasonably well, including popular tools, personalities, authorities, books, websites, and communities. We're also members of several of those communities, free and paid, which means we can ask questions, share perspectives, and get feedback directly from others. That will be critical for validating our initial ideas. We're also our own avatar, meaning we could create something that scratches our own itch, knowing it probably would appeal to others in that PKM community as well. Before we move on, we need to be clear that we still don't have our MVA clearly identified. Instead, we've narrowed where we're looking for our MVA after reviewing the themes we initially identified. We may need to repeat this process several times, finding audiences within audiences within audiences until we found an audience that's small and responsive enough for us to create a precise, attractive solution, and large enough that it could generate enough revenue to make our work financially worthwhile. There is no magic formula to determine audience size and financial viability. However, it's worth reading, or rereading, Kevin Kelly's 1000 True Fans to Better Understand the Nuances. Keep in mind that the MVA you choose initially does not have to be the audience that supports your business forever, this is an important insight. You're starting small to make it easier to understand the potential audiences' needs and craft an offer that resonates deeply with that audience. Initially, you want the most responsive audience possible. That's where you'll get the best feedback and the highest likelihood of making sales. Over time, you'll expand into new audiences as your business grows. Once we've identified this smaller initial audience, the next step is to start creating some structure for our ideas related to our audience's potential needs. That's a critical difference between discover and validate. Discover is an exercise in taking in information and looking for patterns. It's driven entirely by what others are saying they want to accomplish, want to experience, or want to avoid. Validate is when we as creators begin to take a more active role connecting what we've learned about our audience with our interests, skills, and experience. We'll transition from focusing on our audience's problems to developing, sharing, and getting feedback on our ideas for potential solutions. Again, we'll use Project X as an example. One of the themes we identified in our research was a desire to generate aha moments on demand. We also know that generating ideas is a very compelling reason people become interested in personal knowledge management. We both joined a five-week program in January, led by Nick Milo, not for research specifically, but because we are deeply curious learners and continuously driven to hone our learning process. Everyone in this cohort paid $622, which is not an insignificant investment. Clearly, these PKM people are motivated to solve a problem they have or a meaningful desire. On the first call, Nick asked, why do you want to make notes? Below are some of the responses. Manage and create knowledge. To create content for my community. To put my ideas in order. Learn lots of things and remember things. Idea generation. To preserve my epiphanies. I want to make notes because I need to save my thoughts and memories, track my thoughts and memories, connect my thoughts and memories, make sense, make decisions, easily accomplish projects on time. Being surprised at myself. Growth. Capture ideas and connect them to make new ideas. Make sense of the world. Improve intelligence. Learning. Remember and refer. Learning and retrieval of information. Visualize my creativity. Remember things I have learned. Create better content. Developing a highly functioning ecosystem of my knowledge. Connections. To retrieve ideas. Skill equals collect, connect, practice reps equals making sense, mastery. Legacy for my son. To make sense of information. Staying agile. To remember. To generate new perspectives. To make ideas more visual. To increase the velocity of my projects. Some of our skills, expertise and experience intersect perfectly with that audience's desire. We're both fascinated by the creative process and we have studied creativity in depth. There's an underlying framework for dramatically increasing the likelihood of generating creative insights and we use that framework every day. If we were right and could teach others how to generate aha moments on demand, that seems like it would be very valuable to the PKM audience and very likely other audiences within the creative space. However, we don't yet know if that's true. We think it could be, and broadly speaking, there are two ways we could find out. Option number one would be to get really excited about the idea, create a course, build the infrastructure to sell and deliver that course, and then start buying traffic in hopes that our hunch was correct. We might get lucky and be right, and that might be time well spent. However, we might be wrong. Remember, broadly speaking, 80% of offers fail, which means it's probably not a great idea to spend a lot of time and energy creating something to sell if we're not yet convinced anyone wants to buy it. That leads us to option number two, taking our ideas for a framework that generates aha moments on demand directly to the personal knowledge management audience and getting their feedback. We want to know if it resonates, and, if it does, how intensely. We also want to be open to the possibility that we're on the right track, but our audience has even better ideas. For example, we might hear someone say, yes, generating aha moments on demand would be helpful, but what I really want to accomplish is, or we might hear objections. That's too good to be true. No one can generate ideas on demand. We also might discover that we're too late to the party. For example, if someone were to say, Oh, that's interesting. Have you read so and so book where she describes the process? There's so much nuance to be uncovered and keeping an open mind is critical. Finally, it's entirely possible that you'll get mixed signals. Some people may love your ideas, and others may not. If that happens, look for any patterns among the two groups. What characteristics, if any, are shared by people who respond enthusiastically versus those who don't? If you can isolate those variables, they may point the way to a more specific MVA comprised of people who really engage with your idea. This is another example of how we're continuously stacking probabilities in our favor. One last thought before we wrap up this module. Approach validation from the perspective of wide open curiosity. It's really easy to fall in love with an idea and want the world to fall in love with it too. It's also really easy to fool yourself with confirmation bias, focusing on the feedback that supports your idea and discounting everything else. We're all human and we can fool ourselves in predictable ways. We highly recommend Michael Lewis's book, The Undoing Project, if you're curious about some of the many ways we consistently fool ourselves. To combat these tendencies, keep an open mind and capture whatever feedback you get. Ask questions, dig deeper, stay open to possibilities. If you do that, you're far more likely to identify themes that will lead to compelling offers and faster business growth. In part two, we'll explain how to externalize, refine, and distill your ideas before you begin to share those ideas with a potential MVA for feedback. Part two will be a roll up your sleeves workshop lesson. You'll be writing to reveal insights that will make everything else you do invalidate easier. Questions or feedback, please use the comments below. Use the private accountability community to have discussions with your peers and share your insights with them. Andre and Sean.
